text: The laparoscopic view reveals bilateral femoral hernias in the patient, with the robotic setup prepared for repair.
The laparoscopic view shows the right femoral hernia, which appears larger than the left femoral hernia.
After examining the surgical field laparoscopically, the surgeon prepares to dock the XI robotic system, positioning three ports in the upper abdomen for the procedure.
The surgeon holds a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and a pair of scissors in the right hand, preparing to perform surgical maneuvers on the bilateral femoral hernia.
The surgeon manipulates a piece of fat in the pelvic region, pressing it upward to ensure it remains out of the visual field during the procedure.
The surgeon begins the robotic bilateral femoral hernia repair on the left side, using the laparoscopic view to guide the procedure.
The surgeon creates a standard peritoneal flap by making a vertical, somewhat oblique incision, followed by a transverse horizontal incision.
The surgeon navigates within the preperitoneal plane, avoiding entry into the retrorectus plane, while manipulating tissue layers in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon navigates to the space of Retzius, an area located behind the pubic bone, as part of the dissection process.
The surgeon carefully maintains the retrorectus fat layer while approaching the Cooper's ligament during the dissection.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut the medial umbilical ligament, which was inhibiting the movement of the right arm during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers laterally in the space of Bogros, closely hugging the peritoneum to maintain precise dissection.
The surgeon carefully progresses towards the deep pelvis, navigating through the surgical field with visible precision.
The surgeon uses effective retraction to expose and manipulate the cotton candy-like areolar tissue, facilitating the safe development of the surgical space.

The surgeon focuses on achieving hemostasis while dissecting deep into the pelvis near the femoral vessels, obturator structures, and vas deferens, ensuring careful separation to avoid injury to critical anatomical structures.
The surgeon is deep in the pelvis, focusing on the upper pelvis structures before proceeding further. The anatomy appears normal, which is unusual in patients with inguinal hernia.
The surgeon advances deeper into the pelvis, focusing on the herniated femoral contents, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
A large piece of yellow adipose tissue is visible extending into the femoral space, with the surgeon considering whether to reduce or leave it in place.
The surgeon observes preperitoneal fat herniating into the femoral space, noting its appearance and location within the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the large femoral vein, which transitions into the external iliac vein, within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully searches for the natural plane between the bladder and lateral structures, as well as between the peritoneum and lateral structures, within the surgical field.
The surgeon applies tension and counter tension to reveal the edge of the peritoneum, enhancing visibility of the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates lateral space in the surgical field to accommodate the placement of a mesh overlay, ensuring adequate coverage over the anatomical structures.
The edge of the peritoneum is clearly visible, delineating the boundary between the peritoneal cavity and surrounding structures.
A small hole is visible in the peritoneum, which the surgeon plans to repair later.
The surgeon positions the mesh deep within the space of Retzius, ensuring it is laid securely in the anatomical area.
The obturator nerve is visible within the surgical field, identifiable as a distinct structure among surrounding tissues.
The surgeon navigates deeper into the surgical field, moving beyond the visible edge of the peritoneum.
The surgeon uses minimal cautery near the obturator nerve, carefully avoiding excessive thermal spread to protect surrounding structures.
The obturator artery becomes visible in the surgical field, positioned near the previously identified obturator nerve.
The surgeon continues the dissection in a clear surgical field, with no blood staining the tissue, allowing for straightforward visualization of anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues to peel back tissue layers, maintaining close proximity to the perineum, revealing underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon aims to position the mesh deeply, transitioning from the deep pelvis to the lateral pelvis, ensuring no bladder or peritoneum is behind it. This requires careful skeletonization of the vas deferens.
The medial umbilical ligament is visible and begins to obstruct the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the medial umbilical ligament, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the fat over Cooper's ligament and places stitches near the vas deferens to secure the mesh and prevent it from riding up.
The surgeon removes fat tissue over Cooper's ligament to expose the ligament for suturing, ensuring clear visibility of the anatomical structure.
On the opposite side, the surgeon decides to leave the fat intact, using palpation to visualize Cooper's ligament through the overlying fat.
The surgeon decides not to divide the fat over Cooper's ligament, maintaining the existing tissue structure in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes lymphatic leakage from nodal structures and applies clips to control the leakage.
The surgeon navigates medially and posteriorly, revealing the medial umbilical ligament in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a structure that is not the ureter, noting its medial position relative to the vas deferens.
The surgeon divides the medial umbilical ligament, although it did not obstruct the surgical field in this patient.
The surgeon clips the vessel within the medial umbilical ligament, which may still contain blood, on the main side to prevent potential pain.
The surgeon completes dissection on the left side and prepares to begin dissection on the larger right side.
The surgeon begins the incision near the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), creating an oblique vertical limb before extending the incision across the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to stay outside the retroactive space, avoiding entry into deeper tissue layers.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in developing the surgical field on the right side, possibly due to a higher anatomical position compared to the left side.
The surgeon displays the flap on one side, highlighting its differences compared to the flap on the opposite side.
The surgeon performs dissection by transitioning from the visceral space adjacent to the peritoneum into the parietal space near the rectus muscle, dividing the attenuated posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon exposes two distinct layers of tissue resembling a typical posterior rectus sheath above the arcuate line.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon leaves a significant amount of retrorectus fat intact, provided it is not excessively thick, during the dissection process.
The surgeon joins the dissection completed on the left side, connecting the previously dissected areas within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a potential indirect inguinal hernia, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies an additional interparietal hernia in the patient, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces a small amount of incarcerated fat and prepares to cover it with mesh used for repairing the primary hernia.
The surgeon reduces the fat in the lateral area and prepares to reposition closer to the peritoneum.
The surgeon navigates between the peritoneum and the extraperitoneal fatty tissue, maintaining a clear separation between these anatomical layers.
The surgeon repositions into the surgical plane adjacent to the peritoneum, navigating between the peritoneum and extraperitoneal fatty tissue.
The surgeon identifies a structure that is likely the vas deferens within the surgical field.
A small amount of fat is visibly herniating lateral to the inferior gastric vessels.
The surgeon navigates medial to the internal ring, adjusting the position relative to the peritoneum and surrounding fatty tissue.
The surgeon begins dissecting the femoral contents, focusing on separating these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon extracts the contents from the femoral hernia, revealing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon identifies and manipulates the herniated fat around the gastric area, which is visibly protruding.
The surgeon proceeds smoothly through the tissue layers, aided by effective hemostasis, which minimizes bleeding and allows for clear visualization of the surgical field.
The femoral hernia sac is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides both medium vocal ligaments, which were obstructing the surgical field.
The video shows a small area of scarring tissue, possibly related to previous surgical manipulation or anatomical changes.
The medial umbilical ligament is visibly drawn into the femoral hernia, indicating its involvement in the herniation.
The surgeon successfully extracts the contents from the femoral hernia, revealing the tip of the hernia contents.
The surgeon begins to search for the cord lipoma, carefully inspecting the surgical field for the presence of the lipoma within the cord structures.
The surgeon identifies fatty tissue, likely a lipoma, within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully teases apart the tissue to locate the natural capsule surrounding the suspected lipoma, using gentle manipulation to avoid bleeding.
The surgeon carefully separates the lipoma from the testicular vessels, ensuring clear distinction between the fatty tissue and the vascular structures.
The surgeon has separated an obvious lipoma and identifies another fatty structure surrounding the spermatic cord, indicating the presence of additional lipomatous tissue.
The surgeon continues dissection to differentiate between the testicular vessels and the cord lipoma, carefully teasing apart the fatty tissue structures.
The surgeon visually identifies the vas deferens, located beneath the surrounding fatty tissue and lipoma.
The surgeon identifies the testicular vessel running within the surrounding fatty tissue, attempting to differentiate it from adjacent structures.
The surgeon successfully avoids damaging the testicular vessels while dissecting around the fatty tissue and cord lipoma.
The surgeon examines a section of fatty cord tissue, noting its resemblance to hernia-like behavior.
The surgeon separates the fatty tissue from the sacrum vessels, carefully distinguishing between the two structures.
The surgeon navigates through retroperitoneal vascular attachments associated with the cord lipomas, carefully dissecting these structures.
The surgeon disconnects the retroperitoneal vascular attachments from the cord lipoma, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies a larger piece of tissue consisting of retroperitoneal fat and decides to keep it attached to its retroperitoneal attachments.
The surgeon places a mesh anterior to the previously manipulated tissue.
The surgeon observes tethered fat that needs to be disconnected to lay the mesh on the cord structures, and continues immediate dissection to achieve this.
The surgeon manipulates a tongue of peritoneum, likely preparing to adjust or dissect it in relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon prepares to remove a tissue sling, likely involving retroperitoneal fat, from its attachments.
The surgeon continues to separate the peritoneum, extending the dissection as far back as possible, revealing underlying structures and tissues.

The surgeon uses the left hand to retract tissue, revealing the anatomical plane and a large packet of nodal tissue on the left side. The nodal tissue is left in place, while the longest obturator artery is visible, extending deep into the space of Retzius.
The surgeon attempts to position the mesh deeply within the surgical field, aiming for optimal placement.
The surgeon identifies a tissue containing a dense network of lymphatics, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to identify a natural tissue plane between the bladder and the lymphatic tissue, leaving the nodal tissue intact.
The surgeon navigates deep into the obturator space, revealing anatomical structures within the pelvic region.
The surgeon has developed a space within the obturator area, leaving the obturator fat in place, to potentially accommodate the mesh deeply if required.
The obturator internus muscle is visible in the surgical field, located deep within the obturator space.
The surgeon begins to skeletonize the vas deferens, carefully exposing and isolating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon observes a large amount of peritoneum attempting to move behind the mesh within the obturator space.
The camera provides a clear view into the femoral space, revealing the anatomical area prepared for potential mesh placement.
The surgeon visually identifies a large vein within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies additional peritoneum in the surgical field, which may need to be managed or repositioned.
The surgeon peels back the tissue, revealing underlying anatomical structures and creating a clearer view of the surgical field.
The surgeon ensures that the peritoneum is securely positioned to prevent it from slipping behind the mesh in the femoral space.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the underlying structures, ensuring that the peritoneum cannot slip behind it.
The obturator nerve is clearly visible in the surgical field, surrounded by adjacent anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the femoral branch and the genitofemoral nerve, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates adequate space for the placement of surgical mesh, ensuring sufficient room within the surgical field.
The surgeon provides a view into the thermal space, revealing anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the interparietal hernia, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a flat sheet pro grip mesh, measuring 20 by 15 centimeters, and trims it to the appropriate size for placement over the interparietal hernia.
The surgeon uses a tape measure to assess the dimensions of the space surrounding the interparietal hernia, preparing for the placement of a custom-sized mesh.
The surgeon displays the first piece of mesh that was cut, showing its size and shape in relation to the surgical field.
The surgeon places a trimmed piece of mesh into a large space, visibly noting that the mesh appears small relative to the size of the space.
The surgeon places the mesh into the surgical site to assess its fit and coverage within the large space.
The surgeon demonstrates the ability to move and remove the piece of mesh from its placement within the surgical field.
The surgeon evaluates the placement of a mesh piece, noting that while it provides good posterior and deep femoral coverage, it appears too short for the medial area.
The surgeon assesses the mesh placement, noting that the mesh appears insufficiently wide for the surgical area, leaving gaps in coverage.
The surgeon places the mesh obliquely rather than horizontally, adjusting its orientation within the surgical field.
The surgeon measures a 20 by 15 centimeter piece of mesh, trimming the corners to ensure better posterior placement.
The surgeon trims the mesh posterior laterally to achieve better coverage and fit within the surgical site.
The surgeon observes the transition from the Bagros space to the space of Retzius, noting the need for additional mesh placement to prevent the peritoneum from protruding.
The surgeon positions the mesh to cover the fatty tissue over the psoas muscle, ensuring adequate coverage.
The surgeon rolls the mesh back up, preparing to remove it from the surgical field as a new mesh is introduced.
The surgeon exchanges the mesh, preparing for its placement in the surgical field.
The surgeon presents a newer piece of mesh, preparing for its placement in the surgical field.
The surgeon displays a wider piece of mesh, preparing to place it within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to undo the mesh slightly, preparing it for easier unfurling during the procedure.
The surgeon positions the newly exchanged mesh over the surgical area, demonstrating its improved coverage of the femoral space.
The probe grip adheres firmly to the tissue surface, demonstrating its secure attachment during the procedure.
The surgeon positions the mesh deep into the metropubic space, approximately three centimeters deep, ensuring it covers the area effectively.
The surgeon observes the positioning of the mesh, noting that if it rises slightly, the femoral space will remain covered and not exposed.
The surgeon unfolds the mesh over the surgical area, demonstrating the deployment technique that ensures optimal coverage of the space.
The surgeon folds the mesh across the surgical field, ensuring it is properly positioned over the tissue.
The surgeon folds the mesh completely before proceeding to the next fold, ensuring it is properly positioned within the surgical field.
The surgeon covers an unusual interparietal hernia with a mesh, ensuring it is securely placed over the hernia site.
The surgeon identifies Cooper's ligament and positions the mesh over it, visible through the surrounding fat, preparing to anchor the mesh securely in that area.
The surgeon adjusts the mesh by dragging it lower to achieve more overlap over the lateral femoral space.
The surgeon observes a portion of the mesh that is folded, adjusting its position within the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the mesh to achieve better coverage over the femoral space, ensuring it is properly positioned and visible through the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Polysorb suture to secure the mesh to Cooper's ligament, visible through the mesh and surrounding fat tissue.
The surgeon observes a wide direct defect and secures it at two points using sutures, ensuring stability and coverage.
The surgeon focuses on the periosteum and Cooper's ligament, likely preparing to secure or manipulate these structures.
The surgeon secures the mesh in place to prevent it from riding up and exposing the posterior aspect of the femoral space, ensuring stable fixation.
The surgeon visualizes Cooper's ligament through the surrounding adipose tissue.
The surgeon places a stitch near a visible vessel, highlighting the proximity of the vessel to the suture site.
The surgeon demonstrates the area where the mesh adheres effectively, ensuring stable fixation within the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the adherence of the mesh in various anatomical locations, including posterior, lateral, superior, and medial areas, ensuring stable fixation across the surgical field.
The mesh is visibly secure in its placement, with no signs of displacement or exposure in the surgical field.
The obturator nerve is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the coverage of the interparietal hernia, ensuring it is adequately secured within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the seam of the anatomical mesh within the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the anatomical mesh in a left-right orientation to ensure adequate coverage over the surgical area.
Clear fluid is observed trickling down from the divided fatty tissue.
The surgeon places clips on the fatty tissue, securing it in position.
The distal side of the surgical field is observed to be draining a small amount of clear fluid.
The surgeon applies clips to the side of the fatty tissue where clear fluid is observed trickling down.
After placing the clip on the tissue, the leakage of clear fluid becomes more pronounced, visibly highlighting the area of concern.
The surgeon places an additional clip on the tissue to address an area that was previously unclipped, aiming to prevent further leakage.
The surgeon unfurls the remaining portion of the mesh, ensuring it is properly positioned over the surgical area.
The surgeon unfolds the mesh, ensuring the lower flap is completely free, and places it down to achieve good coverage over the surgical area.
The surgeon ensures that the mesh has good posterior overlap, indicating proper placement and coverage over the surgical area.
The surgeon places two stitches in the mesh, securing it in position within the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the area, revealing visible blood vessels through the tissue.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flaps, folding and suturing the tissue to complete the closure of the surgical site.
